section.title: Taste and See That the Lord Is Good
section.sectionRationale: An acrostic psalm of David blessing the LORD at all times, inviting others to taste and see His goodness, instructing children in the fear of the LORD, and closing with the promise that the LORD protects the righteous so completely that not one of their bones will be broken, words the Gospel of John identifies as fulfilled at the cross, John 19:36.
section.thesis: David's invitation to taste and see that the LORD is good, and his testimony that God is near to the brokenhearted and protects the righteous so that not one bone is broken, finds its fullest meaning in Jesus Christ, the true Passover Lamb whose bones remained unbroken at the cross.
section.frameNote: Six movements: blessing the LORD at all times (1-3); seeking the LORD and being answered, the angel of the LORD encamping around those who fear Him (4-7); the invitation to taste and see that the LORD is good (8-10); wisdom instruction for children in the fear of the LORD (11-14); the LORD's eyes on the righteous and nearness to the brokenhearted (15-18); and the closing promise of deliverance, unbroken bones, and redemption (19-22).

units.label: I will bless the LORD at all times

verses.text: I will bless the LORD at all times; His praise will always be on my lips.
My soul boasts in the LORD; let the oppressed hear and rejoice.
Magnify the LORD with me; let us exalt His name together.

units.label: The angel of the LORD encamps
units.note: seeking the LORD and being answered

verses.text: I sought the LORD, and He answered me; He delivered me from all my fears.
Those who look to Him are radiant with joy; their faces shall never be ashamed.
This {{ani|poor}} man called out, and the LORD heard him; He saved him from all his troubles.
The angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear Him, and he delivers them.

units.label: Taste and see that the LORD is good
units.note: an invitation to experience God's goodness

verses.text: {{taam|Taste}} and see that the LORD is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in Him!
Fear the LORD, you His saints, for those who fear Him lack nothing.
Young lions go lacking and hungry, but those who seek the LORD lack no good thing.
Come, children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the LORD.
Who is the man who delights in life, who desires to see good days?
Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from deceitful speech.
Turn away from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it.

units.label: The LORD is near to the brokenhearted

verses.text: The eyes of the LORD are on the righteous, and His ears are inclined to their cry.
But the face of the LORD is against those who do evil, to wipe out all memory of them from the earth.
The righteous cry out, and the LORD hears; He delivers them from all their troubles.
The LORD is near to the brokenhearted; He saves the contrite in spirit.

units.label: Not one of them will be broken

verses.text: Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the LORD delivers him from them all.
He protects all his bones; not one of them will be {{shabar|broken}}.
Evil will slay the wicked, and the haters of the righteous will be condemned.
The LORD redeems His servants, and none who take refuge in Him will be condemned.

ani.gloss: Poor, afflicted, lowly. David identifies himself with this term, not as a title of shame but as the honest condition from which the LORD hears and delivers, a pattern the Messiah Himself embraces, Isaiah 53:7, Luke 4:18.

taam.gloss: To taste, perceive, experience directly. David's invitation is not merely to believe facts about God's goodness but to experience it firsthand, an invitation echoed in 1 Peter 2:3, quoting this very verse of those who have tasted that the Lord is good.

shabar.gloss: To break, shatter. God's promise that not one bone of the righteous will be broken, verse 20, is quoted directly by John as fulfilled when soldiers found Jesus already dead and did not break His legs, John 19:33-36, echoing also the Passover lamb regulation, Exodus 12:46.

basic.context: David resolves to bless the LORD at all times, inviting the oppressed and all who hear to rejoice with him. He testifies that seeking the LORD brought deliverance from fear, and that the angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear Him. He invites everyone to taste and see the LORD's goodness for themselves, teaches children the fear of the LORD as the path to a good life, and describes God's nearness to the brokenhearted. The psalm closes celebrating that though the righteous suffer many afflictions, the LORD delivers them completely, even protecting their very bones from being broken, and redeems His servants from condemnation.
basic.hermeneutics: This acrostic psalm should be read as both personal testimony and wisdom instruction, moving naturally from praise, to invitation, to teaching, showing that experiential knowledge of God's goodness, not merely correct doctrine about Him, is what the psalm calls every reader to taste for themselves.
basic.originalLanguages: David identifies himself as ani, poor and afflicted, the honest condition from which the LORD delivers him, invites others to taam, taste, God's goodness directly rather than merely hearing about it, and closes promising that not one bone of the righteous will shabar, break, a promise John identifies as fulfilled at the cross, John 19:36.
basic.typology: David's identification as the poor and afflicted one whom the LORD hears anticipates the Messiah's own embrace of lowliness, Isaiah 53:7, Luke 4:18, and the promise that not one bone of the righteous will be broken, verse 20, anticipates both the Passover lamb regulation, Exodus 12:46, and its fulfillment in Jesus, whose legs were not broken at the crucifixion, John 19:33-36.
basic.christ: John explicitly quotes this psalm's promise, that not one bone of the righteous would be broken, as fulfilled when Roman soldiers, finding Jesus already dead, did not break His legs as they did the two criminals crucified with Him, John 19:33-36. This same detail echoes the Passover lamb, of which no bone was to be broken, Exodus 12:46, identifying Jesus as the true Passover Lamb whose sacrifice delivers His people. And where David invites others to taste and see the LORD's goodness, verse 8, Peter applies this directly to those who have tasted that the Lord is good in Christ, 1 Peter 2:3.
basic.god: God is shown as intimately near to the brokenhearted and contrite, attentive to the cries of the afflicted, and so thorough in His protection of the righteous that even their bones are kept from breaking, a promise of care extending to the smallest details of suffering.
basic.crossReferences: John 19:33-36 quotes this psalm's promise of unbroken bones as literally fulfilled at the crucifixion of Jesus. Exodus 12:46 commands that no bone of the Passover lamb be broken, a regulation John connects directly to Jesus as the true Passover Lamb. 1 Peter 2:3 quotes this psalm's invitation, 'you have tasted that the Lord is good,' applying it to believers' experience of Christ.
basic.oneStory: David's invitation to taste and see the LORD's goodness, and his testimony that God protects the righteous so completely that not one bone is broken, find their fullest meaning in Jesus Christ, the true Passover Lamb whose unbroken bones at the cross fulfill this very psalm, and in whom all who taste His goodness are redeemed from condemnation.

context.literary: An acrostic psalm of David following the order of the Hebrew alphabet, moving from personal praise and testimony, through a direct invitation to experience God's goodness, into wisdom instruction for children, and closing with a broader reflection on suffering, protection, and redemption.
context.historical: Its traditional superscription connects this psalm to David's escape from Abimelech (or Achish) by feigning madness, 1 Samuel 21:10-15, a moment of desperate vulnerability that gives weight to David's testimony of God's deliverance from fear and trouble.
context.canonical: Belongs to the wisdom tradition within the Psalter, teaching the fear of the LORD as the path to a good life, and its promise of unbroken bones becomes uniquely significant in John's Gospel as a direct fulfillment text connecting the psalm to the crucifixion and the Passover lamb typology.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To testify from personal deliverance that the LORD is good and worthy of praise at all times, to invite others into that same experiential knowledge, and to instruct the next generation in the fear of the LORD as the way to a flourishing life.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own experience of deliverance and praise; it functions prescriptively both as an invitation to taste God's goodness personally and as direct wisdom instruction for children and all who would seek a good life.

debates.question: Is the promise in verse 20, 'not one of them will be broken,' primarily a general statement of God's providential care over the righteous in ordinary life, or does its explicit New Testament quotation in John 19:36 mean it functions as a specific messianic prophecy that was always intended to point to Christ's crucifixion?

views.label: The verse is best read first as a general promise of God's providential protection over the righteous person's life and body, part of the psalm's broader wisdom teaching, which John then applies typologically to Jesus as the ultimate righteous sufferer in whom the pattern reaches its highest and most literal fulfillment
views.case: The psalm's immediate context, verses 19-22, speaks broadly of the righteous facing many afflictions from which the LORD delivers them, a pattern of general pastoral comfort rather than a narrowly predictive statement about a single future individual, and John's use of it reflects the common New Testament pattern of finding deeper, Christ-centered fulfillment in Old Testament patterns rather than isolated predictions.

views.label: Because John explicitly quotes this verse as fulfilled, using language of prophecy fulfillment, 'these things took place that the Scripture might be fulfilled,' John 19:36, the verse should be read as carrying built-in prophetic weight toward the ultimate righteous sufferer, with David's own experience serving as a partial, lesser instance of a pattern whose complete meaning was reserved for Christ
views.case: John's explicit fulfillment language elevates this specific detail beyond mere pastoral application, suggesting the Holy Spirit intended this psalm's language, alongside the Passover lamb regulation, Exodus 12:46, to converge specifically and prophetically in the crucifixion account.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the ultimate, complete fulfillment of this promise is found in Christ; the question is simply whether David's own experience already fully satisfied the promise as one link in a pattern, or whether the promise was always aimed beyond David toward the Messiah, with either view affirming the same conclusion drawn in John's Gospel.

study.originalLanguages: David identifies himself as ani, poor and afflicted, invites others to taam, taste, God's goodness directly, and closes promising that not one bone of the righteous will shabar, break, a promise John identifies as literally fulfilled at the cross, John 19:36.

typology.type: David the poor and afflicted one whom the LORD hears, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: The Messiah's own embrace of lowliness and suffering, Isaiah 53:7, Luke 4:18.

typology.type: Not one bone of the righteous broken, verse 20
typology.fulfillment: The Passover lamb of which no bone was broken, Exodus 12:46, and Jesus, whose legs were not broken at the crucifixion, John 19:33-36.

study.christ: John explicitly quotes this psalm's promise, that not one bone of the righteous would be broken, as literally fulfilled when Roman soldiers found Jesus already dead and did not break His legs, John 19:33-36. This detail identifies Jesus as the true Passover Lamb, of whom likewise no bone was to be broken, Exodus 12:46, whose sacrifice delivers His people from death. And where David invites others to taste and see the LORD's goodness, verse 8, Peter applies this directly to believers who have tasted that the Lord is good in Christ, 1 Peter 2:3.
study.god: God is shown as intimately near to the brokenhearted and contrite, attentive to the cries of the afflicted, and so thorough in His protection of the righteous that even the smallest details of their suffering, down to their very bones, are held within His care.

crossReferences.note: Quotes this psalm's promise of unbroken bones as literally fulfilled when soldiers found Jesus already dead and did not break His legs.
Commands that no bone of the Passover lamb be broken, connected by John directly to Jesus as the true Passover Lamb.
Quotes this psalm's invitation, 'you have tasted that the Lord is good,' applying it to believers' experiential knowledge of Christ.

study.oneStory: David's invitation to taste and see the LORD's goodness, and his testimony that God protects the righteous so completely that not one bone is broken, reach their fullest meaning in Jesus Christ, the true Passover Lamb whose unbroken bones at the cross fulfill this very psalm, and in whom all who taste His goodness are redeemed.

sermon.title: "This Poor Man Cried"
sermon.why: Begg's meditation on Psalm 34:6 shows that pride keeps us from bringing our troubles humbly to the Lord, and that when we earnestly and humbly call on Christ, following David's example, He hears us and answers.

quiz.q: What does David resolve to do in verse 1?

options.text: Bless the LORD at all times, with His praise always on his lips.
options.note: This opening resolution sets the tone for the whole psalm's ongoing, not merely occasional, praise.

options.text: Praise the LORD only after every trouble ends.
options.note: The resolution is to praise at all times, not to wait until difficulty passes.

options.text: Praise the LORD often, but only when he personally feels like it.
options.note: Close, since frequent praise is right, but verse 1 commits to praise 'at all times,' regardless of feeling, not conditioned on his mood.

options.text: Praise the LORD only in the temple.
options.note: The resolution is for continual, all-times praise, not limited to a specific location.

quiz.q: What invitation does David extend in verse 8?

options.text: Taste and see that the LORD is good.
options.note: This is the psalm's central, most quoted invitation to experience God's goodness directly.

options.text: Believe correct facts about God's goodness without necessarily experiencing it.
options.note: Close, since right belief matters, but 'taste and see' specifically calls for direct, personal experience, not mere intellectual assent to facts.

options.text: Judge whether the LORD is good based on others' testimony alone.
options.note: The invitation is to personal, direct experience, tasting and seeing, not secondhand testimony alone.

options.text: Wait until death to know if God is good.
options.note: The invitation is for present, immediate experience of God's goodness.

quiz.q: What does David teach children in verses 11-14?

options.text: The fear of the LORD, including keeping the tongue from evil and turning from evil to do good.
options.note: This section is explicit wisdom instruction aimed at the next generation.

options.text: General good manners toward others.
options.note: Close, since kind conduct is part of the teaching, but the instruction is specifically rooted in the fear of the LORD, not manners considered apart from reverence for God.

options.text: How to acquire wealth quickly.
options.note: The teaching concerns the fear of the LORD and righteous speech and conduct, not wealth acquisition.

options.text: How to avoid ever speaking to anyone.
options.note: The instruction is about controlling speech to avoid evil and deceit, not avoiding speech altogether.

quiz.q: According to verse 18, where is the LORD especially near?

options.text: To the brokenhearted, saving the contrite in spirit.
options.note: This verse specifically identifies God's nearness to those crushed in spirit.

options.text: To all people equally, regardless of their emotional or spiritual state.
options.note: Close, since God's care extends broadly, but verse 18 specifically highlights a special nearness to the brokenhearted and contrite, not an undifferentiated nearness to everyone.

options.text: Only to kings and rulers.
options.note: The nearness described is to the brokenhearted broadly, not limited to political leaders.

options.text: Nowhere in particular; God is equally distant from all.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's claim about God's special nearness to the brokenhearted.

quiz.q: What promise does verse 20 make, and where is it quoted as fulfilled in the New Testament?

options.text: That not one of the righteous person's bones will be broken, quoted as fulfilled at the crucifixion in John 19:36.
options.note: John explicitly connects this promise to the soldiers not breaking Jesus's legs at the cross.

options.text: That the righteous will be kept safe from serious harm, referenced generally in the Gospels.
options.note: Close, since safety is the broader theme, but the specific promise concerns bones not being broken, and its precise New Testament quotation is in John 19:36, not a general Gospel reference.

options.text: That the wicked will have their bones broken as punishment, quoted in Romans.
options.note: The verse's promise concerns protection of the righteous, not judgment on the wicked, and its New Testament quotation is in John's Gospel.

options.text: That believers will be physically immortal in this life.
options.note: The promise is specifically about bones not being broken, not general immortality in this present life.

questions: David resolves to bless the LORD at all times, not only when circumstances are good. What would it look like for you to praise God specifically in whatever difficulty you are facing right now?

David invites others to taste and see that the LORD is good, an experiential knowledge, not mere information. Where in your life have you settled for knowing facts about God rather than genuinely tasting His goodness?

David teaches children to keep their tongue from evil and deceit, and to seek peace and pursue it. What would it look like for you to actively pursue peace this week rather than simply avoiding open conflict?

The psalm says the LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves the contrite in spirit. Where are you brokenhearted right now, and how does knowing God draws especially near in that state change how you bring it to Him?

John shows this psalm's promise of unbroken bones fulfilled at the cross, connecting Jesus to the Passover Lamb. How does seeing Christ as the true Passover Lamb, sacrificed for you, deepen your understanding of the deliverance this psalm celebrates?

translationNotes.note: This poor man likely refers to David himself, using the term ani, poor or afflicted, as an honest description of his condition when he cried out and was heard.
Taste and see renders taam, to perceive by direct experience, quoted by Peter in 1 Peter 2:3 to describe believers' experiential knowledge of Christ's goodness.
Not one of them will be broken is quoted directly in John 19:36 as fulfilled at the crucifixion, and echoes the Passover lamb regulation of Exodus 12:46 that no bone of the lamb be broken.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 34:1-22. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of truthforlife.org.
